---
title: "corpus-caesarianum-authorship"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

This is an [R Markdown](http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com) Notebook. When you
execute code within the notebook, the results appear beneath the code.

```{r include=TRUE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(results = "hold")
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = '~/PycharmProjects/corpus-caesarianum-authorship')
#knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = '~/GitHub/corpus-caesarianum-authorship')
```

# Data Preparation

```{r Libraries}


library(factoextra)
library(dplyr)
library(ggrepel)
library(tidyverse)
library(cluster)
library(combinat)
library(dendextend)
library(MASS)
library(multcomp)
library(ggtext)
library(printr)
```

Note that I was learning dplyr and tidyverse as I created this file, so
there are some confusions I should clear up here.

- tidyverse loads several other packages, including tibble

- tidytable uses tidyverse syntax over data.table

- dplyr is a syntax for working with data frames, including tibbles

- The `%>%` pipe is not identical to the `|>` pipe.

  - The former is from the magrittr package, the latter is native to R

  - The former lets you change the position of the piped-in argument by
    replacing an argument with a dot (.) R's `|>` allows this, but with
    \_ as the placeholder and the argument must be named

  - You can access members of a piped output with `%>%`, so
    `data.frame %>% mean(.$column)` works with magrittr but not base R.
    dplyr::pull() can do this with the base R pipe, though

(For the above ggrepel library, see
<https://github.com/slowkow/ggrepel>)

```{r Get postagged_only.csv. Big data!}

dims <- c(
  "title", 
  "author", 
  "book",
  "section",
  "path",
  "form",
  "lemma",
  "tag",
  "Aspect",
  "Mood",
  "Number",
  "Person",
  "Tense",
  "VerbForm",
  "Voice",
  "Case",
  "PronType",
  "Gender",
  "Polarity",
  "Degree",
  "NumType",
  "Deprel",
  "parent_form",
  "parent_lemma",
  "parent_tag",
  "parent_Aspect",
  "parent_Mood",
  "parent_Number",
  "parent_Person",
  "parent_Tense",
  "parent_VerbForm",
  "parent_Voice",
  "parent_Case",
  "parent_PronType",
  "parent_Gender",
  "parent_Polarity",
  "parent_Degree",
  "parent_NumType",
  "parent_Deprel"
)

data <- read.csv("postagged-texts.csv", na.strings = "", col.names = dims, stringsAsFactors = TRUE)

#This isn't a ratio or count variable
data <- data |>
  mutate(Person = as.factor(Person))

summary(data)
```

Add book numbers, if applicable. I add them to a separate column and the
title; a bit unclean, but I'll have to fix it later.

```{r eval=FALSE}

get_book <- function(title, cite1, cite2) {
  if (title == "De bello Gallico") {
    return(substr(x=cite2, start=1, stop=1))
  }
  else if (title == "De Bello Civili") {
    return(cite1)
  }
  else {
    return('0')
  }
}

data <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(book = get_book(title, cite1, cite2)) |>
  relocate(book, .before = form)

# data <- data |>
#   rowwise() |>
#   mutate(title = paste(title, "_", book))
# 
# data <- tidytable::select(data, -book)
```

Now let's get the section number.

```{r eval=FALSE}
get_sec <- function(title, cite1, cite2) {
  if (title == "De bello Gallico") {
    return(unlist(
      strsplit(as.character(cite2), '[.]')
    )[2])
  }
  else if (title == "De Bello Civili") {
    return(cite2)
  }
  else {
    return(cite1)
  }
}

data <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(section = get_sec(title, cite1, cite2)) |>
  relocate(section, .before = form)
```

The data needs to be split up by section first, then put back into
books. The way I recommend doing this is appending both the book and
section number to the title to get this result: "*title*\_*x_x(x)*".
Then, we use the same code blocks below as in the Letters to Atticus,
feeding this new title instead of the base one.

Let's modify the titles first:

```{r}

data <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  mutate(title = paste(title, "_", book, "_", section))
```

The following two code blocks come from the [cltk-2025-atticus
repository](https://github.com/cubis182/cltk-2025-atticus "GitHub Repo"),
and they transpose the data so that each column is a feature value
instead of a feature type and the values in the columns are the number
of times each feature appears.

```{r Atticus-processing-1}

# # Rename "Imp" to "Imperative" in mood category
# levels(data$Mood) <- c(levels(data$Mood), "Imperative")
# sequence <- which(data$Mood == "Imp")
# data$Mood[sequence] <- "Imperative"
# remove(sequence)
# 
# data$Mood[data$Mood == "Imp"] <- "Imperative"
# data$Mood <- droplevels(data$Mood) # Remove the old "Imp

#Turn it into a function
replace_value <- function(column, to_replace, replace_string) {
  # Rename "Imp" to "Imperative" in mood category
  levels(data[[column]]) <- c(levels(data[[column]]), replace_string)
  sequence <- which(data[[column]] == to_replace)
  data[[column]][sequence] <- replace_string
  remove(sequence)
  
  data[[column]][data[[column]] == to_replace] <- replace_string
  data[[column]] <- droplevels(data[[column]]) # Remove the old "Imp
  return(data)
}

data <- replace_value("Mood", "Imp", "Imperative")
data <- replace_value("Mood", "Ind", "Indicative")
data <- replace_value("parent_Mood", "parent_Ind", "parent_Indicative")
data <- replace_value("parent_Mood", "parent_Imp", "parent_Imperative")

#Remove unneeded variables
data <- data |> tidytable::select(-NumType, -parent_NumType, -Degree, -parent_Degree)
data <- data |>
  filter(tag != 'PUNCT')

#An argument could be made for removing Polarity, but I'll wait to see how common it is and how much variance there is between the texts

```

## Subsetting Book/Section Numbers

I want a simple way to subset book as section numbers, with a function
that combines only the desired pieces together. Book AND section isn't
currently possible, just the following combinations:

- Commentary

- Book

- Section

- Commentary and book

- Commentary, book, and section

- Book and section

## Simple Variable Counts

Now that we have the data, what we're *really* interested in is how many
of each tag appears in each work (how many of each POS, how many perfect
verbs, whatever). The point is, we're not interested in every individual
word.

NOTE: I've created a heading (currently empty) down below where we test
formatting variables like Gorman 2016.

This block of code will loop through and create a new data frame called
***final.frame*** (which is probably poorly named, but I'll fix it later
or I won't). This data frame has a variable for the count of each tag,
and every row is a unique work.

```{r Atticus-processing-Create final.frame}

titles <- unique(data$title)
num_words <- table(data$title)
final.frame <- data.frame(title = titles)

for (i in 1:length(titles)) {
    frame <- data[data$title == titles[i],]
    frame <- tidytable::select(frame, title, tag:parent_Deprel & !parent_form & !parent_lemma) #Leave off everything unnecessary except for the title
    for (n in 2:length(frame)) {
      t <- table(frame[, ..n])
      # t <- prop.table(t)
      names <- names(t)
  
      for (name_index in seq(1, length(names))) {
        final.frame[i, names[name_index]] <- unname(t[names[name_index]])
      }
    }
}

# Normalize by number of words in each work
for (i in 2:length(final.frame[1, ])) {
  for (n in 1:length(final.frame[, 1])) {
    final.frame[n, i] <- final.frame[n, i] * (10000 / num_words[[final.frame[n, ]$title]])
  }
}
```

A few things will make our life easier. We don't want any NAs in there,
because that'll gum up the clustering. We'll replace any missing values
with 0, because 0 occurrences of a variable is the only reason there
would be NA values at this stage.

The numbers aren't normalized according to the size of their respective
works yet, so I've divided each number by the total number of words in
the given work in the next chunk. I multiplied each by 100,000 too, just
to get numbers that look a little nicer and aren't in scientific
notation.

Finally, we do what I said above, turning the rownames into the title. I
shorten them to 12 characters max.

```{r Clean up final.frame}
# Removed punctuation because PCA showed that it was an "important" variable
final.frame <- subset(final.frame, select = c(-PUNCT, -X))

# Clean up NAs
final.frame[is.na(final.frame)] <- 0

#Add rownames
# 
# for (i in 1:length(final.frame$title)) {
#   final.frame$title <- as.character(final.frame$title)
#   final.frame$title[i] <- paste(as.character(i), final.frame$title[i])
# }
# 
# rownames(final.frame) <- final.frame$title
# final.frame$title <- lapply(X = final.frame$title, FUN = substr, start = 1, stop = 16)
# rownames(final.frame) <- lapply(X = rownames(final.frame), FUN = substr, start = 1, stop = 16)
```

Let's save this to a file so we can open it in another RMarkdown file.

```{r}

write_csv(final.frame, file = "./feature_values.csv")
```

## Gorman-Style Variables

Step one of this process is to extract common feature pairs.

The number of feature values is `length(final.frame[1,]`.

That's a huge number, so let's start by working with the original data.

```{r}
length(data[1,])
```

Gorman considered only number of base features, not number of parent
features, in feature selection. So, he was considering 20 features,
while I consider 40, with the number doubled. The frequency of a feature
in a parent is not the same as frequency in the whole corpus, so, since
the frequency of a parent feature can be a variable, it should be
treated just like any other variable. What I should do is try it both
ways, of course.

First, let's winnow accounting only for the main tags.

Let's get all combinations of two tags

```{r eval=FALSE}
library(combinat)

two_select_combinations <- combn(colnames(data |> tidytable::select(tag:parent_Deprel)), 2)

length(two_select_combinations)
```

Okay, that's 992 different combinations. We need to:

1.  Turn the pairwise_combination matrix into a tibble

    ```         
    two_select_combinations <- as_tibble(two_select_combinations)
    ```

2.  Apply the following to every combination in the list

3.  Count the number of rows in the data frame that don't have **NA** in
    either category

    - Note that selecting those two rows is as easy as the following
      line of code:

      ```         
      data |> tidytable::select(pairwise_combinations[,1])
      ```

    - Just plug in the column number and the output works in the select
      function

4.  In that column of the pairwise_combinations tibble, add a third
    field with the count retrieved in the previous step.

5.  Sort the tibble by the third field, cull out a percentage based on
    Gorman 2016

```{r eval=FALSE}



# 
# #Return TRUE for every row in the tibble that has an NA either in column 1 or 2
# no_na <- function (tib) {
#   len <- length(tib)
#   
#   #Go through and get a vector of bools showing whether each item in the row is an NA
#   bools <- unlist(lapply(tib, is.na))
#   
#   #If the sum of the vector of bools is 0, then none are NA
#   return(sum(bools) == 0)
# }

# variable_count <- function(matrix) {
#   
#   subset_of_data <- data |> 
#     tidytable::select(matrix)
#   
#   #example: tidytable::select(data, two_select_combinations[,1])
#   
#   #Get a logical vector of how many rows in the selected columns
#   #have no NAs
#   features_exist <- subset_of_data |>
#     dplyr::rowwise() |>
#     no_na()
#   
#   #TRUE is equal to 1 and FALSE to 0, so summing the vector
#   #will return the number of rows which have an NA in neither
#   #column
#   return(sum(features_exist)) 
#     
# }

combination_freqs <- apply(X=two_select_combinations, MARGIN = 2, FUN=variable_count)

tib_two_select_combinations <- as_tibble(two_select_combinations)

tib_two_select_combinations[3,] <- as.list(as.character(combination_freqs))

tib_two_select_combinations

#Use apply and pass variable_count to an apply function over the columns of the matrix titled two_select_combinations
```

Let's try to transpose this tibble so we can actually make one column
numeric:

```{r eval=FALSE}
tib_two_select_combinations <-
  tib_two_select_combinations |> 
  mutate(type = c('tag1', 'tag2', 'num')) |> 
  #Note that the default names all start with V (V1, V2, V3...Vn)
  pivot_longer(starts_with("V")) |> 
  pivot_wider(names_from = type, values_from = value) |> 
  select(!name) |>
  mutate(across(num, as.integer))

tib_two_select_combinations

arrange(tib_two_select_combinations, num)

```

Now that we have a list of features, let's eliminate any combinations
that occur zero times together

```{r eval=FALSE}
tib_two_select_combinations <- tib_two_select_combinations |> filter(num > 0) |> arrange(num) 

tib_two_select_combinations
```

I'm interested in seeing whether there is any resemblance to normality;
I would expect not, but I want to visualize the data.

```{r eval=FALSE}

ggplot(tib_two_select_combinations, aes(num)) + geom_histogram(binwidth=200)
library(dgof)

ks.test(jitter(tib_two_select_combinations$num, amount = 0.01), "pnorm")
```

The null hypothesis is that the distribution is the same as the normal
distribution, so we can soundly reject that, not that it was in
question.

Okay, so, we've gotten data on all the combinations of two variables,
but what about 3 to 5? Let's refactor the above code into a function we
can re-use:

```{r}

if (!("combinat" %in% (.packages()))) {
  library(combinat)
}

#Filters a row to rows which have ZERO NA values in them
no_na <- function(tib, vec) {
  if(NA %in% vec) {
    return(tib)
  }
  tib <- filter(tib, .data[[vec[1]]] != FALSE)
  vec <- vec[2:length(vec)]
  return(no_na(tib, vec))

}


variable_count <- function(matrix, source_data) {
  
  subset_of_data <- source_data %>% 
    tidytable::select(all_of(matrix)) %>%
    no_na(colnames(.))
  
  return(nrow(subset_of_data)) 
    
}

#combinations_n <- 2

#combn, data, tag, parent_Deprel, 
variable_combinations <- function(combinations_n, source_data) {
  select_combinations <- combn(colnames(source_data |> tidytable::select(tag:parent_Deprel & !parent_form & !parent_lemma)), combinations_n)
  
  #length(select_combinations)
  
  combination_freqs <- apply(X=select_combinations, MARGIN = 2, FUN=variable_count, source_data = source_data)
  
  tib_select_combinations <- as_tibble(select_combinations)
  
  #Get the length of the column
  col_len <- nrow(tib_select_combinations)
  
  #Add a new row with the frequency each one occurs
  tib_select_combinations[(col_len + 1),] <- as.list(as.character(combination_freqs))
  
  #Time to take out the trash!
  remove(col_len)
  
  #transpose
  
  tib_select_combinations <-
    tib_select_combinations |> 
    #The number of columns depends on the $ of combinations, hence the code in the following line
    mutate(type = c(letters[1:combinations_n], 'num')) |> 
    #Don't select 'num'
    pivot_longer(!type) |> 
    pivot_wider(names_from = type, values_from = value) |> 
    dplyr::select(!name) |>
    mutate(across(num, as.integer))
  
  arrange(tib_select_combinations, num)
  
  tib_select_combinations <- tib_select_combinations |> filter(num > 0) |> arrange(num) 
  
  return(tib_select_combinations)
}


```

I've realized that counting up feature-value pairs at the end of this
file is proceeding too slowly, so I need to sample from the full
dataset.

```{r}

data_backup <- data

data <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  #inelegant, but it works
  mutate(commentary = paste(unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[1], unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[2])) |>
  ungroup() |>
  dplyr::slice_sample(n = 1000, by = commentary)

data$commentary <- as.factor(data$commentary)
```

```{r message=FALSE}
#Setting message option to false makes sure as_tibble's annoying .name_repair option doesn't print every single repaired name

#Let's test it

system.time({
  two_combinations <- variable_combinations(2, source_data = data)  
})

  #   user  system elapsed 
  # 7.673   0.284   8.735 

```

Another piece of information we're interested in is whether a variable
occurs in multiple works. This situation is different from Gorman 2016,
as he was working with a much larger corpus. We have only five works. I
say we cull the lower quartile.

Another note: I'm gonna remove feature combinations that never occur
together BEFORE culling the lower quartile.

```{r}
#DON'T RUN THIS MORE THAN ONCE IN A SESSION...

#NOTE: I don't get rid of zeroes because the variable_combinations function already removes them
cull_quartile <- function(combinations, quartile = "25%") {
  print(paste("Length before culling: ", as.character(length(combinations$a))))
  
  #Get rid of features which occur in less than 20% of the corpus
  filtered <- combinations |> filter(num > (nrow(data) / 5))
  
  print(paste("Length after culling: ", as.character(length(filtered$a))))
  return(filtered)
}



two_combinations <- two_combinations |> cull_quartile()
```

```{r}
one_combinations <- variable_combinations(1, data)

#one_combinations <- one_combinations |> cull_quartile()
```

How expensive would it be to brute force it this way for every
combination? What about three to five? (NOTE: These numbers are
inflated, as they include the parent_lemma and parent_form, two columns
the data actually excludes)

```{r}
combn(colnames(data |> tidytable::select(tag:parent_Deprel)), 3) |> length()

combn(colnames(data |> tidytable::select(tag:parent_Deprel)), 4) |> length()

combn(colnames(data |> tidytable::select(tag:parent_Deprel)), 5) |> length()
```

The following block gets every combination of 3 variables and uses the
profiler to see how long we spend in each function. Naturally, the
function that checks for NA's takes the longest by far, which is how it
should be.

```{r message=FALSE}
#library(profvis)

#profvis({
  three_combinations <- variable_combinations(3, data)
#})

#    user  system elapsed 
# 110.418   0.276 112.086 

three_combinations <- three_combinations |> cull_quartile()
```

It looks like three is doable, and four, if it's roughly 10 times
larger, should take about 20 minutes. The only one that's really
unreasonable is the combination of 5, so for that one we'll split it up.
I could limit to 1,000 tokens like Gorman does, but we have more leeway.
I'll give 1,000 tokens randomly selected from each book.

```{r eval=FALSE}

#For each work, let's get 1,000 random tokens. Use dplyr::slice_sample

data_sample <- data |>
  rowwise() |>
  #inelegant, but it works
  mutate(commentary = paste(unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[1], unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[2])) |>
  ungroup() |>
  dplyr::slice_sample(n = 1000, by = commentary)

unique(data_sample$commentary)

```

Let's get the samples of five from this one. Even after optimizing a
little and reducing the number, it takes about 20 minutes.

```{r message=FALSE}


system.time({
  five_combinations <- variable_combinations(5, data)
  })

five_combinations <- five_combinations |> cull_quartile()




```

Let's see how fast my computer deals with a combination of 5 without
culling.

```{r eval=FALSE}

system.time({
  five_combinations <- variable_combinations(5)
})

#     user   system  elapsed 
# 1736.510    7.177 1765.962 
```

Hm, about 10 minutes longer. Let's stick with the reduced one

Now, get the combinations of four variables. In this one, I'm going to
use the profiler

```{r message=FALSE}


four_combinations <- variable_combinations(4, data)

four_combinations <- four_combinations |> cull_quartile()
```

The next challenge is that we have a list of all the feature types to
look at, but now we need to cull to the most common feature-value pairs,
including simplex, duplex, triplex, quadruplex, and quinqueplex
variables.

The step-by-step process should be:

1.  Iterate row-by-row over the tibbles of variables
2.  Select a subset of data using the row from the tibble
3.  Iterate row-by-row over the subset and concatenate all the values in
    the row to a string
4.  Add the string to a vector where all the other strings will go
5.  To get the counts, use table(). Cull out the least common ones

We need to work our way backwards from these results. First, we create a
data frame where each row is the string split version of each row in the
vector created above, so that each row in the data frame is a different
combination of features with no repeats.

Now we iterate over all the sections of interest. Each section will be a
row in the new data frame. For each row in the frame created in the
previous paragraph, we count up how many times that combination of
features occurs in the targeted section. We create a column with that
variable name, add the count, and then do this for all variables. Repeat
for each section.

Test with a single variable

```{r eval=FALSE}

test_var <- one_combinations[1]

var_combos <- 
  data |>
  dplyr::select(all_of(test_var)) |>
  unite("pasted", everything(), sep = "_")

table(var_combos) |> as_tibble()
```

Now let's test with two:

```{r eval=FALSE}
test_var <- unlist(transpose(two_combinations[1,1:2]))

var_combos <- 
  data |>
  dplyr::select(all_of(test_var)) |>
  unite("pasted", everything(), sep = "_") |>
  filter(str_detect(pasted, pattern = "NA", negate = TRUE))

table(var_combos) |> as_tibble()
```

Okay, let's put that into a function which, for each row of one of the
combination tables, it lists all the combinations in a table.

```{r}

#Requires a row from a tibble that only includes valid column names and no numerics / other data types
get_var_combos <- function(var_combn, source_data) {
  #browser()
  var_combos <- 
    source_data |>
    dplyr::select(all_of(as.character(var_combn))) |>
    #Added next line to avoid expensive string comparisons
    #dplyr::select(where(all(~ !is.na(.x)))) |>
    #Paste the whole row together
    unite("pasted", everything(), sep = "_") |>
    filter(str_detect(pasted, pattern = "NA", negate = TRUE)) #Remove NAs
  
  if (length(var_combos$pasted) > 0) {return(table(var_combos) |> as_tibble())}
  else {return(NULL)}
}
```

Now we would need to iterate over every row in the tibble.

```{r eval = FALSE}

#Remove the last row

test <- two_combinations |> dplyr::select(!last_col())
list_combn <- apply(test, MARGIN = 1, get_var_combos, source_data = data) |> list_rbind()
```

That worked, so lets put it into a function.

**I'm going to temporarily require a count of more than 30**

```{r}

count_var_combos <- function(var_combn, source_data) {
  selected_var_combn <- var_combn |> dplyr::select(!last_col())
  #browser()
  list_combn <- apply(selected_var_combn, MARGIN = 1, get_var_combos, source_data = source_data) |> list_rbind()
  
  #Make sure to remove zeroes
  return(list_combn)
}

#count_var_combos(two_combinations, data)
```

Now, lets create a function that gets one long tibble with every single
variable in it.

```{r}

run_combos <- function(source_data, title_str) {
  #browser()
  
  all_vars <- bind_rows(
    count_var_combos(one_combinations, source_data),
    count_var_combos(two_combinations, source_data),
    count_var_combos(three_combinations, source_data),
    count_var_combos(four_combinations, source_data),
    count_var_combos(five_combinations, source_data),
  )
  
  all_vars <- all_vars |>
    mutate(title = title_str) #The titles should all be the same...
  
  return(all_vars)
}

#mode: whether to divide the data into commentaries, books, or chapters
get_vars <- function(mode = "book") {
  if (mode == "book") {
    #browser()
    source_data <- data |>
      rowwise() |>
      mutate(title = unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[1:2] |> paste(collapse="_"))
  }
  else if (mode == "commentary") {
    source_data <- data |>
      mutate(title = unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[1])
  }
  else if (mode == "chapter") {
    source_data <- data |>
      mutate(title = paste( 
        unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[1], 
        unlist(strsplit(title, "_"))[3], 
        sep="_")
        )
  }
  else {
    return(NULL)
  }
  
  # browser()
  
  all_vars <- source_data |>
    group_by(title) |>
    group_map(~ run_combos(.x, .y))
  
  return(all_vars |> bind_rows())
}
```

```{r}
#Restore the full data
data <- data_backup
#
remove(data_backup)
remove(data_sample)

all_vars <- get_vars("book")
```

Okay, now we need to transpose the data.

```{r}
all_vars <- pivot_wider(all_vars, names_from = pasted, values_from = n)
```

The number of variables is way too high, so we go by Gorman 2020's
method of only keeping ones that occur in more than 20% of words (n \>
(\# of rows in data) / 5)). **However, for testing purposes, let's first
just get the top 1,000 variables**

Keep columns where sum of column is greater than that figure:

```{r}

limit <- nrow(data) / 5
title <- all_vars$title
culled_all_vars <- all_vars |> dplyr::select(-title) |> 
  dplyr::select(where(~ sum(.x, na.rm = TRUE) > limit))

culled_all_vars <- culled_all_vars |> mutate(title = unlist(title)) |> relocate(title, .before = 1)

```

Now save this to a CSV!

```{r}
write_csv(culled_all_vars, file = "./feature_values_gorman.csv", col_names = TRUE)
```
